title: Author tests for cross-team data visibility and injection vulnerabilities in ProjHub API

ground_truth_issues.description: A test verifies that enumerating child resources of a cross-boundary parent is also denied — the access boundary must propagate through the resource hierarchy.
A test verifies that a participant cannot read a leaf resource by direct identifier when the parent resource belongs to a different organisational boundary.
A test verifies that write operations on cross-boundary resources are denied — a participant must not be able to modify state belonging to another boundary.
A test verifies that destructive operations on cross-boundary resources are denied — a participant must not be able to delete or remove state belonging to another boundary.

A test verifies that resource detail responses returned to non-privileged callers do not include fields that are reserved for administrative use. Sentinel values seeded into those fields must not appear anywhere in the response body.

A test verifies that a non-privileged caller's view of discussion threads is filtered — entries marked for internal/administrative audiences must be absent both structurally and in raw response content.
A test verifies that an administrative event log endpoint returns an access-denied response when called by a non-administrative caller.
A test verifies that an administrative aggregate reporting endpoint returns an access-denied response when called by a non-administrative caller.

A test verifies that a keyword search endpoint is not vulnerable to second-order data extraction via query injection. A crafted payload that would exploit naive string interpolation must not cause privileged data from the underlying store to appear in the response.

A test verifies that a diagnostic endpoint exposing credential information is inaccessible to both unauthenticated and non-administrative callers, and that known credential values do not appear in any response from that endpoint.
A test verifies that system introspection endpoints exposing runtime configuration and environment state are inaccessible to both unauthenticated and non-administrative callers.

A test verifies that a non-privileged PATCH request on a project-level resource cannot overwrite any of the administrative metadata fields — all fields in the payload must be rejected or silently ignored together.

A test verifies that an outbound-request endpoint validates the target URL against private network ranges. Multiple address classes (loopback, link-local metadata, RFC-1918) must all be rejected without making a delivery attempt.

A test verifies that error responses from the application are sanitised — stack traces, server file-system paths, and runtime version strings must not appear in either 404 or 500 error response bodies.

A test verifies that a token-generation endpoint produces values that are not derivable from the input resource identifier. Common deterministic derivation functions must be checked and ruled out; tokens for different inputs must also differ.

An anti-overblock test verifies that an administrative caller retains full cross-boundary list visibility and that privileged metadata fields are present in administrative responses after any access-control fix is applied.

An anti-overblock test verifies that a same-boundary non-admin participant can still read their own resources (list, detail, leaf) — returning 200 for all three access patterns after access-control fixes are applied.

An anti-overblock test verifies that a same-boundary participant can still perform create operations — task creation, comment posting, and attachment upload must all succeed in their own boundary after fixes.
An anti-overblock test verifies that a same-boundary participant can still invoke the duplication, sharing, and export action endpoints on their own resources.
A test verifies that a diagnostic endpoint that exposes a complete data-store snapshot is inaccessible to unauthenticated and non-administrative callers.

A test verifies that task-level action endpoints — task assignment and task status-transition — return access-denied responses when the acting caller does not belong to the project's organisational boundary. Both action endpoints must be exercised against the same cross-boundary task in a single test.

A test verifies that project-level action endpoints — project duplication and project export — return access-denied responses when the acting caller is from a different organisational boundary than the target project. Both action types must be tested against the same cross-boundary project resource.

A test seeds a unique sentinel string into the administrative metadata fields of a task resource via an admin write, then verifies that a non-privileged detail read of that task returns a response body from which the sentinel string is entirely absent — the assertion must operate on raw response content rather than field-key presence alone.

A test seeds unique sentinel values into a project's administrative configuration fields via an admin write, then invokes the project share-link endpoint as a non-member caller and verifies that the response body does not contain those sentinel values in any field — the share payload must not bypass field-level redaction enforced on regular project reads.
